topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen glowed with stark, unforgiving text. '14 Willow Creek Lane. Priced to Sell.' My breath hitched, a shallow, rapid thing in my chest. I reread the listing, the familiar layout of the old kitchen, the overgrown garden visible in the grainy photograph—it was all there, a perfect, aching echo of everything I’d lost. My hands started to tremble, the cheap ceramic mug in my grip suddenly feeling slick and alien. I clicked the link to the agent's contact information, my fingers hovering over the keyboard as if the keys might burn my skin. Why now? It felt like a deliberate, cruel trick played by fate. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, my heels clicking a frantic rhythm against the hardwood floor. Every shadow in the room seemed to deepen, stretching into menacing shapes. I needed to call someone, to ground myself in something solid, but the phone felt too heavy to lift.

[story 2]

I stared at the webpage, my eyes darting back and forth across the property details. The familiar scent of dust and old wood, the smell of my grandmother’s lavender soap clinging faintly to the air in my memory, flooded my senses, overwhelming me. A coldness began to seep into my limbs, making my skin prickle beneath my thin sweater. I wiped a clammy hand across my forehead, trying to clear the sudden fog. It was impossible. This place, this anchor to my earliest years, was being offered up for transaction. I leaned against the window frame, the glass cool against my cheek, and watched the street below with a hyper-focused intensity. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my neck. I took a deep, ragged breath, tasting stale air, and then forced myself to close the browser tab, the sound of the click feeling like a monumental effort.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my desktop, a stark headline demanding attention. My stomach lurched; it felt like a knot of cold wire tightening in my abdomen. I didn't move from my chair, just sat there, rigid, watching the numbers flicker on the screen. Each digit seemed magnified, each potential price tag a heavy stone settling onto my chest. I started tapping my fingers rapidly on the desk, a nervous, uneven percussion against the silence of the apartment. I kept glancing towards the door, half-expecting someone to walk in, to challenge the reality of the listing. My throat felt constricted, dry and tight. I wanted to smash the computer, to erase the evidence, but my hands remained stubbornly still, locked in a state of intense, internal bracing.
